text.title: Code Review Principles
text.summary: Five core principles, the five-type comment taxonomy with blocking rules, and six review categories with concrete checks per category.

list.item: Review the code, not the author. Comment on the code behavior and design, never on the developer's choices as a person.
Keep PRs small and focused. Agent review quality drops sharply above 400 lines changed.
Provide timely feedback: first review within 4 hours of PR opening.
Be constructive and specific. Every BLOCKING comment must include a concrete suggested fix.
Use reviews as learning opportunities for both reviewer and author.

rule.statement: Use exactly five comment types in every code review: BLOCKING, SUGGESTION, NITPICK, QUESTION, PRAISE. Authors must resolve all BLOCKING comments before merge. SUGGESTION and NITPICK are non-blocking.
rule.rationale: Ambiguous comment intent is the primary source of review friction. When reviewers leave comments without explicit type, authors must guess whether a comment is blocking or advisory. This uncertainty delays merges and causes unnecessary back-and-forth.

list.item: BLOCKING: bugs, security issues, standard violations — author must fix before merge.
SUGGESTION: improvements that would help but are not required — author decides.
NITPICK: style preferences, minor word choices — author can apply or dismiss, no discussion needed.
QUESTION: reviewer does not understand something — not blocking, but author should clarify.
PRAISE: good code that should be recognized and kept in future refactors — do not change this.

rule.statement: Cap NITPICK count at 5 per review. More than 5 nitpicks signal that linting rules are missing, not that the author needs more feedback.
rule.rationale: Excessive nitpicks overwhelm the author and bury the BLOCKING comments that actually matter. If reviewers are producing 20+ nitpicks, the solution is a lint rule, not more review comments.

rule.statement: Cover all six categories in every review: correctness, design, maintainability, testing, performance, security. Never review only the most interesting category.
rule.rationale: Reviewers naturally gravitate toward their domain of expertise. A developer focused on design may produce zero security findings. Explicit coverage of all six categories ensures systematic examination regardless of reviewer preference.

list.item: Correctness: does it work? Edge cases handled? Business logic correct?
Design: well-organized? Follows project patterns? Single responsibility?
Maintainability: readable? Meaningful variable names? Adequate comments?
Testing: sufficient coverage? Tests edge cases? Tests behavior, not implementation?
Performance: N+1 queries? Unnecessary allocations? Blocking operations in hot paths?
Security: input validation? No secrets in code? SQL injection prevented? Auth checks present?
